title: File Handling & Exceptions
description: Learn how to read from and write to files, process JSON, and gracefully handle runtime errors.

tutorial.title: Working with Files
tutorial.content:
Most real-world programs need to read data from files (like logs, configs, or text).
Python provides a built-in `open()` function to interact with files.

The safest way to open a file is using the `with` statement. It automatically
closes the file for you when the block ends, even if an error occurs!

tutorial.title: Reading a File
tutorial.content:
with open('example.txt', 'r') as file:
    content = file.read()
    print(content)

tutorial.title: Reading Methods
tutorial.content:
1. .read()      → Reads the entire file as a single string.
2. .readline()  → Reads just one line at a time.
3. .readlines() → Returns a list where each element is a line.

You can also iterate over the file object directly:
  with open('file.txt', 'r') as f:
      for line in f:
          print(line.strip())

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: Which statement ensures a file is properly closed automatically?

tutorial.title: Writing Data
tutorial.content:
To write to a file, open it in 'w' (write) or 'a' (append) mode.

WARNING: 'w' mode will overwrite the file completely if it already exists!
'a' mode will add your new text to the end of the existing file.

tutorial.title: Writing Example
tutorial.content:
# Write mode (overwrites)
with open('output.txt', 'w') as file:
    file.write('Hello, World!\n')
    file.write('This is a new line.\n')

# Append mode (adds to the end)
with open('output.txt', 'a') as file:
    file.write('This is appended.\n')

tutorial.title: Pro Tip: Encoding
tutorial.content:
When dealing with text files, it's best practice to specify the encoding
(usually 'utf-8'). This prevents errors when your text contains emojis
or special characters.

  with open('file.txt', 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
      f.write('Python is great! 🚀')

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: Which mode should you use to add text to an existing file without deleting its contents?

tutorial.title: What is JSON?
tutorial.content:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a standard format for storing and
transporting data. It looks almost exactly like Python dictionaries and lists!

Python has a built-in `json` module to handle it easily.

tutorial.title: JSON Serialization
tutorial.content:
import json

data = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30, 'active': True}

# Save dictionary to a JSON file
with open('data.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=4)

# Load JSON file back into a dictionary
with open('data.json', 'r') as f:
    loaded_data = json.load(f)
    print(loaded_data['name'])

tutorial.title: dump/load vs dumps/loads
tutorial.content:
Notice the 's' at the end:

json.dump()  / json.load()   → Read/write directly from/to FILES.
json.dumps() / json.loads()  → Convert to/from a STRING (the 's' stands for string).

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: Which function saves a Python dictionary directly into a JSON file?

tutorial.title: What is an Exception?
tutorial.content:
An exception is an error that happens while the program is running.
For example, trying to divide by zero, or trying to open a file that
doesn't exist. Left unhandled, exceptions will crash your program.

tutorial.title: Try / Except
tutorial.content:
try:
    # Code that might cause an error
    result = 10 / 0
except ZeroDivisionError:
    # Code that runs if the error happens
    print('You cannot divide by zero!')

print('Program continues normally...')

tutorial.title: Catching Specific Errors
tutorial.content:
You should catch specific errors rather than all errors blindly.

  try:
      with open('missing.txt', 'r') as f:
          print(f.read())
  except FileNotFoundError:
      print('The file was not found!')

If you use a bare 'except:', it will catch everything, which can
hide real bugs in your code.

tutorial.title: Quick Check
tutorial.question: What is the purpose of the `except` block?

tutorial.title: Else and Finally
tutorial.content:
The `try`/`except` structure can be extended with `else` and `finally`.

  else: runs ONLY if the try block succeeds (no exception).
  finally: runs NO MATTER WHAT (even if an exception happens or the function returns).

tutorial.title: Else/Finally Example
Raising Exceptions
Quick Check
tutorial.question: Which block executes regardless of whether an exception occurred or not?
